course.name: ICTD: Information and Communication Technologies for Development and Sustainability
course.audience: Senior undergraduate, graduate, and PhD students in Computer Science, most with limited prior exposure to development studies or environmental sustainability in rural/developing regions (India-focused)
course.purpose: Build awareness of development and sustainability challenges in rural India, and equip students with a research/innovation pipeline -- from understanding context, to building CS-driven solutions, to evaluating and scaling them responsibly.
course.framework_description: The course content is organized as a matrix of AREAS (application domains for innovation) x TOPICS (research/engineering skill stages that can be applied within any area). Cross-cutting axes (gender, caste, social capital, etc.) run through every cell rather than being a separate area or topic, since they modulate how problems, solutions, and outcomes play out in every domain. A single class or reading may map to multiple areas and/or multiple topics; the matrix is a lens for building a balanced syllabus and reading list, not a rigid partition. Not every (area, topic) cell needs to be covered in a given offering -- the goal is breadth of exposure across most areas and most topics by the end of the course. Each area, topic, and cross-cutting axis now also carries a 'keywords' array -- phrases and terms drawn from the current readings.json corpus plus general domain vocabulary -- intended for filtering candidate papers pulled from venues.json (title/abstract keyword matching as a first-pass relevance filter, to be followed by human review).

areas.name: Agriculture
areas.description: Smallholder farming, agricultural markets and value chains, extension services, input access, land, irrigation, mandis/commodity prices, farmer producer organizations, agri-tech. Covers both the production side (crop choice, land size and productivity, extension advice, weather/climate risk) and the market side (price discovery, procurement, MSP, arrivals, hoarding, supply chains, demonetization shocks). Includes remote-sensing/ML applications to agriculture (crop classification, yield mapping, cropland change) as well as ethnographic and market-structure accounts of smallholder life.

areas.name: Health
areas.description: Rural healthcare delivery, public health systems (e.g. NRHM, ASHA, ICDS), maternal and child health, telemedicine/mHealth, epidemic and disease surveillance, health information systems, and the use of mobile/web/social data to predict or track health outcomes. Includes both delivery-system accounts (community health workers, mobile medical units) and CS-heavy applications (disease modeling, malaria risk mapping, epidemic nowcasting, respiratory illness and pollution linkages).

areas.name: Labour
areas.description: Employment and unemployment, informal labour markets, migration for work, wages, workplace conditions, gig/platform work, labour rights and welfare/public-works schemes (especially MGNREGA), and the use of call-record, social-media, or survey data to measure labour-market flows. Overlaps with forests_restoration and water_security via MGNREGA's dual role as both a livelihood safety net and a vehicle for afforestation/water-harvesting assets.
keywords: MGNREGA (Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act)

areas.name: Governance
areas.description: State capacity and public service delivery, e-governance, transparency and accountability mechanisms (RTI, social audits, grievance redressal), political and corporate power/ownership networks, corruption and rent-seeking, election-cycle effects on public goods provision, public financial systems, open data/open government, smart-city and datafication debates, and urban/informal-settlement visibility through data. Includes both institutional/political-economy accounts and CS applications (nightlights for GDP/electrification proxies, satellite-based urban monitoring, bureaucratic ICT systems).

areas.name: Livelihood
areas.description: Income generation and economic security beyond formal employment: microfinance and financial inclusion, self-help groups (SHGs), cooperatives, informal enterprise, artisanal and non-farm livelihoods, social protection/safety nets, and poverty measurement/targeting using mobile-phone, satellite, or survey data. Includes both the institutional side (SHG/cooperative models, credit access) and the CS-heavy poverty-mapping literature (nightlights, CNN/transfer-learning on satellite imagery, mobile CDR-based wealth prediction).
keywords: poverty prediction from mobile phone data

areas.name: Education
areas.description: Access to and quality of schooling in rural/low-resource contexts, learning outcomes, ed-tech (intelligent tutoring systems, mobile/offline learning), teacher and school accountability, informal/community learning models, digital and data literacy. This is currently the thinnest area in the reading list -- a priority target for the venue-search follow-up.

areas.name: Water Security
areas.description: Groundwater and surface water resources, hydrology, irrigation and micro-irrigation, watershed and tank-cascade systems, water governance, drought and flood exposure, remote sensing for water resources, and water's role in agricultural and livelihood resilience. Spans sensing (measuring aquifers, groundwater storage, surface water extent), modeling (system dynamics, time-series causal analysis of groundwater), and application/governance (community-based groundwater management, tank systems, participatory groundwater governance). Climate change is a cross-cutting dimension here via changing rainfall and warming-driven groundwater depletion.

areas.name: Forests & Restoration
areas.description: Forest cover and deforestation/degradation monitoring, forest carbon (sequestration estimation, carbon credits, offsets, REDD+), forest rights and community/commons-based forest management, afforestation and ecological restoration (including under public-works schemes like MGNREGA), canopy height and biomass estimation, and remote-sensing methods (optical, radar, LiDAR/GEDI, deep learning) applied to forests. Includes both the sensing/estimation literature and the socio-political literature on carbon-offset integrity and forest tenure.

areas.name: Wildlife & Conservation
areas.description: Biodiversity and species distribution modeling, habitat and ecosystem monitoring, camera-trap and citizen-science data, human-wildlife interaction, conservation planning and protected areas. A newer, thinner area in the reading list -- expect most existing entries to come from the 2023 course's 'supplementary topics' (species distribution modeling, tree species biodiversity) and from Climate Change AI's Ecosystems & Biodiversity / Forests tags.
keywords: wasteful spending in tree planting programs

areas.name: Media & Information Ecosystems
areas.description: Community media, citizen journalism, information/voice platforms (IVR, community radio), mass media structure and ownership, media bias, social media adoption and use, information diffusion, homophily and network structure, and the political economy of media in rural/developing contexts. Spans ethnographic accounts of community media platforms (Gram Vaani, CGNet Swara, Khabar Lahariya) and quantitative media/network-analysis work (bias detection, polarization, virality).

areas.name: Infrastructure & Connectivity
areas.description: Electricity access and reliability, roads, water supply infrastructure, telecom/network connectivity and access, community/small-scale cellular networks, and physical/digital infrastructure that underlies delivery of services in every other area. Includes both economic-impact studies (telecom and growth) and CS/networking work (community networks, blackout mapping, electricity demand mapping).

cross_cutting_axes.name: Climate Change
cross_cutting_axes.description: Climate change is treated as a cross-cutting dimension rather than a standalone area: it runs through water security (droughts, groundwater depletion, changing rainfall), forests & restoration (carbon sequestration, offsets, afforestation as mitigation), and wildlife & conservation (habitat shift, species vulnerability), as well as agriculture and livelihood more broadly. Tag readings with this axis whenever climate change -- mitigation, adaptation, or attribution -- is a substantive part of the content, in addition to their area/topic tags.

cross_cutting_axes.name: Gender
cross_cutting_axes.description: How gender shapes access to technology, resources, decision-making power, and how ICT interventions can reproduce or counter gender inequality. Includes women-led media platforms, gendered patterns of technology adoption, empowerment measurement, and ludic/play-based design that surfaces gendered use.

cross_cutting_axes.name: Caste
cross_cutting_axes.description: How caste-based social stratification shapes access, exclusion, and the distribution of benefits/harms of ICT interventions in India. Currently the thinnest cross-cutting axis in the reading list; readings tagged here largely come via assets-based design and marginalized-community media work rather than papers with caste as their primary lens -- a priority target for future search.

cross_cutting_axes.name: Social Capital & Marginalization
cross_cutting_axes.description: Networks, homophily, intermediaries, and other structural social factors (class, religion, geography, disability, etc.) that reproduce or counter marginalization and inequality; who gets left out even when a technology 'works' on average. Includes network-science accounts (homophily, weak ties), data/algorithmic justice framings, and empirical work on urban informal settlements and satellite-based poverty/slum mapping that documents uneven visibility.

topics.name: Problem Discovery
topics.description: Deeply understanding context: the lives, constraints, incentives, and existing systems of people in a given area, in order to arrive at specific, well-grounded problem statements rather than assumed ones. Includes theoretical framing (capability approach, development theory, appropriate technology), grounding data (surveys, ethnography, secondary data, sector primers), and critical reading of what has and hasn't worked before. Typically dominates introductory/context-setting classes and covers many areas at once.

topics.name: Solving with CS Fundamentals
topics.description: Applying strong computer science methods to the discovered problem: data science and statistical analysis, machine learning (including remote sensing/satellite ML, computer vision, NLP, forecasting/time-series), systems and networking, and building robust technical artifacts appropriate to the constraints of the context (connectivity, devices, literacy, language). The largest and most technically dense topic in the current reading list, dominated by satellite/remote-sensing ML methods.

topics.name: Ethnographic Design & Iteration
topics.description: Translating a technical solution into a usable product via participatory/user-centered/ethnographic design principles, and iterating through field testing and pilots. Concerned with fit between technology and lived practice, incentive design, co-design with communities and intermediaries, and design methods specific to constrained/rural contexts (voice interfaces, community radio formats, ludic/play-based design).

topics.name: Socio-technical Dynamics at Scale
topics.description: Understanding what happens once a system is deployed at scale: how errors (including AI/algorithmic errors) are handled, how power and inequality are exacerbated or countered, how intermediaries and bureaucracies mediate the technology, and whether intended positive outcomes are actually materializing in practice. Covers algorithmic/data-justice critiques, bureaucratic subversion and adaptation of ICT systems, and the gap between platform design intent and real-world use.

topics.name: Impact Evaluation
topics.description: Formally evaluating whether an intervention achieved its intended impact, using statistical/causal-inference methods such as randomized control trials (RCTs), difference-in-differences, propensity score matching, regression discontinuity, and theory-of-change/thematic analysis for qualitative impact. Includes both methods papers and applied impact studies (mobile money, market information systems, political-connection effects on outcomes).

topics.name: Operations & Scaling
topics.description: Understanding the operational, institutional, and organizational processes needed to take a validated system from pilot to scale: staffing and training, supply chains, partnerships with government/NGOs/private sector, financing and sustainability models, and long-term maintenance. Currently the thinnest topic in the reading list -- most existing coverage comes from telecenter/community-network sustainability studies and MGNREGA-linked tooling; a priority target for the venue-search follow-up.
keywords: planning and information systems for scale-up

matrix_usage_notes: A single reading/class often spans multiple topics (e.g. a paper on satellite-based poverty mapping is both cs_fundamentals and, if it evaluates policy outcomes, impact_evaluation).
Introductory/context-setting classes at the start of a course typically map to problem_discovery across many areas simultaneously -- this is intentional and expected.
Cross-cutting axes should be tagged wherever a reading substantively engages them, in addition to its area/topic tags, not as a replacement for them.

The matrix is a planning and retrieval tool for building a reading list and schedule; course designers should aim for breadth across most areas and most topics rather than full coverage of every cell.

area_agnostic_topic_vector.name: Problem Discovery
area_agnostic_topic_vector.methodology_description: The general intellectual toolkit for understanding development/sustainability problems before proposing a technology, independent of which area it is later applied to: theories of development and wellbeing (capability approach, freedom-centered development), critiques of growth/inequality (Piketty-style political economy, degrowth/post-development/pluriverse thought), the appropriate-technology tradition (matching technology choice to context and scale rather than assuming 'more advanced is better'), and ICT4D-specific meta-frameworks (the ICT4D manifesto, process approaches to project success, the capability-choice framework for ICT4D). These readings teach a way of thinking about problems and technology strategy that a student should carry into agriculture, health, governance, or any other area -- they are chosen for the general stance they teach, not for a specific application.

area_agnostic_topic_vector.name: Solving with CS Fundamentals
area_agnostic_topic_vector.methodology_description: The general technical toolkit for building solutions, independent of which area's problem it is pointed at: how to choose and scope a technology strategy for the context (Schumacher's appropriate-technology argument belongs here as much as in problem_discovery, because it is fundamentally about technology-choice methodology), general-purpose remote-sensing and machine learning method papers (foundation models, transfer learning, self-supervised learning, vision transformers, generative/diffusion models for imagery) that are introduced via one dataset or domain but are meant to generalize, and general data-mining/statistical methodology (spatiotemporal data mining, generalizable ML pipelines). The test for inclusion here is that the methodology is the point of the reading, not the specific area it happens to demonstrate on.

area_agnostic_topic_vector.name: Ethnographic Design & Iteration
area_agnostic_topic_vector.methodology_description: General design methodology for translating understanding into artifacts and iterating with communities, independent of area: participatory and user-centered design principles, assets-based and culturally-situated design (designing from a community's existing capacities rather than treating them as a deficit), ludic/play-based design as a way of surfacing values and power in a design process, and the general craft of co-design with intermediaries. These readings teach a design stance and set of methods applicable whether the artifact is for agriculture, health, or governance.

area_agnostic_topic_vector.name: Socio-technical Dynamics at Scale
area_agnostic_topic_vector.methodology_description: General frameworks and critical lenses for understanding what happens to a technology once it meets the world at scale, independent of area: theories of datafication and algorithmic/data justice, critiques of smart-city and platform logics, and general accounts of the gap between designer intent and real-world appropriation. Currently thin in the reading list as a purely area-agnostic category -- most sociotechnical-dynamics readings in the corpus are anchored to a specific area (bureaucracy, media, governance) rather than being pure theory, which is itself worth noting when searching for new readings: this topic may benefit from more general theoretical anchors (e.g. classic STS/algorithmic-accountability theory) alongside applied cases.

area_agnostic_topic_vector.name: Impact Evaluation
area_agnostic_topic_vector.methodology_description: The general statistical and methodological toolkit for evaluating impact, independent of area: controlled experimentation and A/B testing methodology, measurement and indicator-framing methodology (e.g. how to structure empowerment indicators), and methodological critiques of evaluation itself (participant/response bias in HCI evaluation). These are chosen because they teach the mechanics and pitfalls of impact evaluation as a discipline, to be applied later to any area's intervention.

area_agnostic_topic_vector.name: Operations & Scaling
area_agnostic_topic_vector.methodology_description: General organizational and institutional theory for taking a system from pilot to scale, independent of area: what capabilities (in the Sen/capability-approach sense) an ICT program needs to sustain itself, and process-oriented theories of what makes ICT4D projects succeed or fail operationally. Also thin as a purely area-agnostic category -- most operations_scale readings in the corpus are tied to a specific program (telecenters, MGNREGA tooling) rather than general organizational theory, which is a gap worth targeting in future search.

area_agnostic_topic_vector_note: In addition to the areas x topics matrix, each topic also has an area-agnostic methodology description and a curated set of example_readings that teach that topic's methodology in a way meant to generalize across areas (e.g. Piketty on inequality, or Sen's capability approach, belong to problem_discovery regardless of whether the reading list is doing agriculture or health that week; Schumacher's appropriate-technology argument belongs to cs_fundamentals because it is fundamentally about technology-choice strategy, even though it predates and never mentions computing). In readings.json, every reading now carries an 'area_agnostic' boolean flag. This is a hint for syllabus building: area-agnostic readings are good candidates for the intro/framing classes at the start of a topic's coverage (before area-specific applications), and for filtering incoming venue papers, a candidate paper matching a topic's methodology_description without matching any area's description/keywords is a plausible area-agnostic addition rather than a mistagged one.
